question: A financial contract that provides its owner with the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell a specified asset at an agreed-upon price on or before a given future date is called a(n) ________ contract.

options.A: option
options.B: futures
options.E: straddle

question: The act where an owner of an option buys or sells the underlying asset, as is his right, is called ________ the option.

options.A: striking
options.B: exercising
options.E: strangling

question: The fixed price in an option contract at which the owner can buy or sell the underlying asset is called the option's:

options.A: opening price.
options.B: intrinsic value.
options.E: time value.

question: The last day on which an owner of an option can elect to exercise that option is referred to as the ________ date.

options.A: ex-payment
options.B: ex-option
options.E: intrinsic

question: An option that may be exercised only on the expiration date is called a(n) ________ option.

options.A: European
options.B: American
options.E: Asian

question: The difference between an American option and a European option is that the American option:

options.A: has a fixed exercise price while the European exercise price can vary within a small range.
options.B: is a right to buy while a European option is an obligation to buy.
options.C: has an expiration date while the European option does not.
options.D: is written on 100 shares of the underlying security while the European option covers 1,000 shares.
options.E: can be exercised at any time up to the expiration date while the European option can only be exercised on the expiration date.

question: An option that may be exercised at any time up to and including its expiration date is called a(n) ________ option.

options.A: futures
options.B: Asian
options.E: American

question: If a call option has a positive intrinsic value at expiration the call is said to be:

options.A: funded.
options.B: unfunded.
options.E: out of the money.

question: A ________ is a derivative security that gives the owner the right, but not the obligation, to buy an asset at a fixed price for a specified period of time.

options.A: futures contract
options.B: call option
options.E: forward contract

question: If you are the owner of a European call option, you:

options.A: are obligated to buy the specified assets prior to the option's expiration.
options.B: have the right to sell the specified assets at the set price at any time up to and including on the expiration date.
options.C: have the right to buy the specified assets at the set price only on the expiration date.
options.D: are obligated to sell the specified assets at the set price if the option is exercised.
options.E: have the right to buy the specified assets at the set price at any time prior to the option's expiration.

question: Which of these will increase the value of a call option? I. An increase in the market value of the underlying asset II. An increase in the option's strike price III. A decrease in the market value of the underlying asset IV. A decrease in the option's strike price

options.A: I and II only
options.B: II only
options.E: I only

question: An out-of-the-money call option is best defined as an option that:

options.A: has an exercise price below the current market price of the underlying security.
options.B: should not be exercised at this time.
options.C: has an exercise price equal to the current market price of the underlying security.
options.E: qualifies as an American option.

question: Jillian owns a call option on WAN stock with a strike price of $20 a share. Currently, WAN is selling for $24.50 a share. Jillian would like to profit on this option but is not permitted to exercise the option for another two weeks. She believes the stock will decline in value before the two weeks is up. What should she do?

options.A: Sell her option today
options.B: Place an order to exercise her option on its expiration date
options.C: Purchase an additional call option on WAN today with a strike price of $20
options.D: Place an order to exercise her option as soon as she is permitted to do so
options.E: Convert her American option into a European option

question: The owner of a European put option has the:

options.A: right, but not the obligation, to sell the underlying asset at the specified price only on the specified date.
options.B: right, but not the obligation, to sell the underlying asset at the specified price during a specified period of time.
options.C: obligation to sell the underlying asset on the specified date, but only if they can do so at the specified price.
options.D: obligation to buy the underlying asset sometime during the specified period at the specified price.
options.E: right, but not the obligation, to buy the underlying asset at the exercise price on the expiration date.

question: An option that grants the right, but not the obligation, to sell shares of the underlying asset during a particular time period at a specified price is called:

options.A: either an American or a European option.
options.B: an American call option.
options.E: a European call option.

question: Which one of the following provides the right to sell a stock anytime during the option period at the strike price even if the market price of the stock declines to zero?

options.A: American call
options.B: European call
options.E: Should the underlying stock price decline to zero, all options are null and void.

question: Which of these will decrease the value of a put option? I. An increase in the market value of the underlying asset II. An increase in the option's strike price III. A decrease in the market value of the underlying asset IV. A decrease in the option's strike price

options.A: I and II only
options.B: I and IV only
options.E: IV only

question: An in-the-money put option is one that:

options.A: has an exercise price greater than the underlying stock price.
options.B: has an exercise price less than the underlying stock price.
options.D: should not be exercised at expiration.
options.E: should not be exercised at any time.

question: On the expiration day, the maximum price of a put option on a stock is the greater of the:

options.A: stock price minus the exercise price, or 0.
options.B: the exercise price or 0.
options.C: exercise price minus the stock price, or 0.
options.E: exercise price or the stock price.

question: Jeff owns an American put option on 100 shares of ABC stock. The option has a strike price of $32.50 and a September expiration date. The stock has recently been declining in value, currently sells for $27.65 per share, and is expected to continue declining in value. Ignore all costs and taxes. If today is Wednesday, August 14, he:

options.A: cannot exercise his option even though he would like to do so.
options.B: should hold his option until September.
options.C: can exercise his option and earn a profit.
options.D: should exercise his option today and then sell the shares of stock on the September expiration date.
options.E: should let his option expire unless the stock price increases above $32.50 a share.

question: A put option on ABC stock with an exercise price of $35 expires today. The current price of ABC stock is $36. The put is:

options.A: funded.
options.B: unfunded.
options.E: out of the money.

question: Which one of the following statements concerning call option writers is true?

options.A: Call option writers promise to purchase shares if the call option is exercised.
options.B: The call option writer has the option, but not the obligation, to purchase shares if the option is exercised.
options.C: The call option writer is betting that the market price of the underlying asset will increase.
options.D: The call option writer receives a cash payment when the option is written.
options.E: The call option writer earns a profit when an in-the-money option is exercised.

question: Eric has an option position on Langdon stock that results in a zero dollar payoff when the stock price is equal to or greater than the option strike price. What did he do to obtain this position?

options.A: Purchased a call option
options.B: Purchased a put option
options.E: No option position would have this result.

question: Assume you are reviewing a table that lists the current stock option contracts and quotes. Which one of these statements would correctly apply to that table?

options.A: If you write a contract you will pay the bid price per share listed in the table.
options.B: If you write a contract you will receive the difference between the bid and ask prices per share.
options.C: The bid price on a specific contract will be higher than the ask price on that same contract.
options.D: To purchase one contract you must pay 1000 times the quoted bid price shown in the table.
options.E: To purchase one contract you must pay 100 times the quoted ask price shown in the table.

question: Assume you are reviewing a table that lists the current stock option contracts and quotes. On a specific contract the table shows an option interest value of 38 and volume of 9. This means that:

options.A: 9 contracts were exercised on that day while 38 more were traded.
options.B: 38 contracts were traded on that day compared to only 9 traded on the previous day.
options.C: 9 contracts were traded on that day at a price of $.38 per share.
options.D: 38 contracts were traded on that day at the bid price and 9 were traded at the ask price.
options.E: 9 of the 38 outstanding contracts traded on that day.

question: The relationship between the prices of the underlying stock, a call option, a put option, and a riskless asset is referred to as the ________ relationship.

options.A: put-call parity
options.B: covered call
options.E: strangle

question: Given an exercise price, time to maturity, and European put-call parity, the present value of the strike price plus the price of the call option is equal to the:

options.A: current market value of the stock.
options.B: present value of the stock minus the price of a put option.
options.C: price of a put option minus the market value of one share of stock.
options.D: value of a risk-free U.S. Treasury bill.
options.E: price of the stock plus the price of the put option.

question: Selling a covered call is equivalent to:

options.A: buying a zero coupon bond and selling a put.
options.B: selling a put and buying an offsetting call.
options.C: buying the stock and selling the call.
options.D: selling a zero coupon bond and buying a put.
options.E: buying a zero coupon bond and buying a call.

question: You can realize the same value as that derived from stock ownership if you:

options.A: sell a put option and invest at the risk-free rate of return.
options.B: buy a call option and write a put option on a stock and also borrow funds at the risk-free rate.
options.C: sell a put, buy a call, and buy a zero-coupon bond.
options.D: lend out funds at the risk-free rate of return and sell a put option on the stock.
options.E: borrow funds at the risk-free rate of return and invest the proceeds in equivalent amounts of put and call options.

question: Put-call parity can be used to show:

options.A: how far in-the-money put options can be.
options.B: how far in-the-money call options can be.
options.C: the precise relationship between put and call prices given equal exercise prices and equal expiration dates.
options.D: that the value of a call option is always twice that of a put given equal exercise prices and equal expiration dates.
options.E: that the value of a call option is always half that of a put given equal exercise prices and equal expiration dates.

question: Which combination is referred to as a protective put? Assume all sales and purchases refer to ABC stock and its option contract.

options.A: Buying 100 shares of stock and writing one put option contract
options.B: Selling a put option contract and buying an offsetting call option contract
options.C: Buying 300 shares of stock and selling three call option contracts
options.D: Buying a put option contract and buying 100 shares of stock
options.E: Buying a put option contract and selling a call option contract with the same strike price and expiration date

question: Assume you purchase one share of a stock and sell a call option on a single share of that same stock with an exercise price of $25. What is the maximum payoff you can realize on this combination?

options.A: The exercise price of $25
options.B: An amount equal to the stock price on the option expiration date
options.C: An amount equal to $25 minus the stock price on the option expiration date
options.D: An amount equal to the stock price on the expiration date plus $25
options.E: Zero

question: Hi-Tech announces a major expansion which causes the price of its stock to increase and also causes an increase in the volatility of the stock price. How will these two reactions affect the value of call options on this stock?

options.A: Both reactions will decrease the value.
options.B: Both reactions increase the value.
options.C: Neither reaction will affect the value.
options.D: The reactions will have offsetting effects on the value.
options.E: The change in volatility will have no effect while the increased stock price will increase the value.

question: New Tek announces a major expansion which causes the price of its stock to increase and also causes an increase in the volatility of the stock price. How will these two reactions affect the value of put options on this stock?

options.A: Both reactions will decrease the value.
options.B: Both reactions will increase the value.
options.C: Neither reaction will affect put option values.
options.D: The reactions will have offsetting effects on the value.
options.E: The change in volatility will have no effect while the increased stock price will decrease the value.

question: A trading opportunity that offers a riskless profit is called a(n):

options.A: put option.
options.B: call option.
options.E: cross-hedge.

question: The value of an option if it were to immediately expire, that is, its lower pricing bound, is called an option's ________ value.

options.A: strike
options.B: market
options.E: intrinsic

question: The maximum value of a call option is equal to the:

options.A: strike price minus the initial cost of the option.
options.B: exercise price plus the price of the underlying stock.
options.E: purchase price.

question: The lower bound on a call's value is defined as the:

options.A: greater of the strike price or zero.
options.B: greater of the stock price minus the exercise price or zero.
options.C: lesser of the strike price or the stock price.
options.D: lesser of the strike price or zero.
options.E: lesser of the stock price minus the exercise price or zero.

question: The lower bound of a call option's value:

options.A: can be a negative value regardless of the stock or exercise prices.
options.B: can be a negative value but only when the exercise price exceeds the stock price.
options.C: can be a negative value but only when the stock price exceeds the exercise price.
options.E: can be equal to zero.

question: The intrinsic value of a call equals the:

options.A: exercise price minus the stock price.
options.B: upper bound of the call's value.
options.C: market price of the call option.
options.D: lower bound of the call's value.
options.E: premium paid to purchase the call.

question: The intrinsic value of a put is equal to the:

options.A: lesser of the strike price or the stock price.
options.B: lesser of the stock price minus the exercise price or zero.
options.C: lesser of the stock price or zero.
options.D: greater of the strike price minus the stock price or zero.
options.E: greater of the stock price minus the exercise price or zero.

question: Which one of the following statements is correct concerning in-the-money option values?

options.A: The value of a put decreases as the exercise price increases.
options.B: The value of a put increases as the price of the underlying stock increases.
options.C: The value of a call decreases as the exercise price increases.
options.D: An increase in the underlying stock price decreases both the value of a put and a call.
options.E: The value of a call decreases as the price of the underlying stock increases.

question: All else held constant, the value of a call decreases when the:

options.A: time to expiration increases.
options.B: risk-free rate of return increases.
options.E: volatility of the price of the underlying stock increases.

question: Which one of the following will cause the value of a call to decrease?

options.A: Lowering the exercise price
options.B: Increasing the time to expiration
options.D: Lowering the risk level of the underlying security
options.E: Increasing the stock price

question: Assume you own both a May 40 put and a May 40 call on ABC stock. Which one of the following statements is correct concerning your option positions? Ignore taxes and transaction costs.

options.A: An increase in the stock price will increase the value of your put and decrease the value of your call.
options.B: Both a May 45 put and a May 45 call will have higher values than your May 40 options.
options.C: The time premiums on both your put and call are less than the time premiums on equivalent June options.
options.D: A decrease in the stock price will decrease the value of both of your options.
options.E: You can never profit on your positions as your profits on one option will be offset by losses on the other option.

question: You own both a May 20 call and a May 20 put. If the call finishes in the money, then the put will:

options.A: also finish in the money.
options.B: finish at the money.
options.D: either finish at the money or in the money.
options.E: either finish at the money or out of the money.

question: An increase in which one of the following will decrease the value of a call option?

options.A: Interest rate
options.B: Exercise price
options.E: Underlying asset price

question: The effect on an option's value of a small change in the value of the underlying asset is called the option:

options.A: theta.
options.B: vega.
options.E: gamma.

question: In the Black-Scholes option pricing formula, N(d1) is the probability that a standardized, normally distributed random variable is:

options.A: less than or equal to N(d2).
options.B: less than one.
options.E: less than or equal to d1.

question: The Black-Scholes option pricing model is dependent on which five parameters?

options.A: Stock price, exercise price, risk-free rate, probability of occurrence, and time to expiration
options.B: Stock price, risk-free rate, probability of occurrence, time to maturity, and variance of the underlying asset
options.C: Stock price, risk-free rate, probability of occurrence, variance of the underlying asset, and exercise price
options.D: Stock price, exercise price, risk-free rate, variance of the underlying asset, and time to expiration
options.E: Exercise price, probability of occurrence, stock price, variance of the underlying asset, and time to expiration

question: The delta of a call measures the:

options.A: time remaining to expiration compared to the option's original maturity.
options.B: change between an option's original value and its current value.
options.C: swing in the price of the call relative to the swing in the underlying stock price.
options.D: ratio of the change in the option price to the change in the time to expiration.
options.E: volatility of the underlying security.

question: You own stock in a firm that has a pure discount loan due in six months. The loan has a face value of $50,000. The assets of the firm are currently worth $62,000. The stockholders in this firm basically own a ________ option on the assets of the firm with a strike price of ________
If you consider the equity of a firm to be an option on the firm's assets then the act of paying off debt is comparable to ________ on the assets of the firm.

options.A: purchasing a put option
options.B: purchasing a call option
options.E: selling a call option

question: For every positive net present value project that a firm undertakes, the equity in the firm will increase the most if the delta of the call option on the firm's assets is:

options.A: equal to one.
options.B: between zero and one.
options.E: equal to minus one.

question: Assume a risky firm has both bondholders and stockholders. If the firm obtains a government loan guarantee on its existing debt, who will gain from this guarantee?

options.A: Existing stockholders only
options.B: Both existing bondholders and stockholders in proportion to the firm's debt-equity ratio
options.C: Existing bondholders and stockholders on an equal basis
options.E: Future stockholders only

question: If you consider bondholders to be the owners of a firm, then those bondholders:

options.A: own a call option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total equity.
options.B: own a put option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total debt.
options.C: have written a put option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total equity.
options.D: have written a call option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total debt.
options.E: own a put option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total assets.

question: If you consider stockholders to be the owners of a firm, then those stockholders:

options.A: own a call option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total equity.
options.B: own a put option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total debt.
options.C: have written a put option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total equity.
options.D: have written a call option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total debt.
options.E: own a put option on the firm with an exercise price equal to the firm's total assets.

question: A purely financial merger:

options.A: increases shareholder value but does not affect bondholders.
options.B: decreases both bondholder and shareholder values.
options.D: increases bondholder value but does not affect shareholder value.
options.E: reduces shareholder value while increasing bondholder value.

question: Shareholders in a levered firm might wish to accept a negative net present value project if it:

options.A: increases the standard deviation of the returns on the firm's assets.
options.B: lowers the variance of the returns on the firm's assets.
options.D: diversifies the cash flows of the firm.
options.E: decreases the risk that a firm will default on its debt.

question: Alicia is considering purchasing a small building with the intent of opening a craft and sewing center. The cost of the building is $49,000 and the cost to open the center is an additional $150,000. If the center is opened and no other competition is nearby the project will provide a hefty return. However, should the vacant building down the street be used for a competitive type entity, then the center could be a losing proposition. Assume the building is purchased but the opening of the center is delayed. This situation would best be described as ________ on a craft and sewing center.

options.A: purchasing a call option
options.B: writing a call option
options.E: exercising a call on the land and writing a put

question: You purchased eight TJH call option contracts with a strike price of $37.50 when the option quote was $.55. The option expires today when the value of TJH stock is $37.10. Ignoring trading costs and taxes, what is your total profit on your investment?
explanation: You will not exercise the call option because the strike price is greater than the stock price. Why pay more than market price to purchase shares? Thus, your loss is equal to the amount paid to purchase the option contracts: Total profit = 8(100)[−$.55 + MAX($37.10 − 37.50, 0)] Total profit = −$440

question: You purchased five WXO 30 call option contracts at a quoted price of $.34. What is your total profit on this investment if the price of WXO is $33.60 on the option expiration date?

You own two call option contracts on ABC stock with a strike price of $15. When you purchased the contracts the option price was $1.20 and the stock price was $15.90. What is the total intrinsic value of these options if ABC stock is currently selling for $14.50 a share?

Last week, you purchased one call option contract on DI stock with a strike price of $32.50 and an option price of $1.05. What is the intrinsic value per share of stock if DI is currently priced at $34.10?
explanation: Intrinsic value = Max($34.10 − 32.50, 0) Intrinsic value = $1.60 The call is in the money.

question: You purchased five CVB call option contracts with a strike price of $40 when the option was quoted at $3.65. The option expires today when the value of CVB stock is $43. Ignoring trading costs and taxes, what is your total profit on your investment?
You purchased two WXO 15 call option contracts at a quoted price of $.08. What is your total profit on this investment if the price of WXO is $14.80 on the option expiration date?
explanation: Total profit = 2(100)[−$.08 + MAX($14.80 − 15, 0)] Total profit = −$16 The option expires out of the money.

question: Three weeks ago, you purchased a July 45 put option on RPJ stock at an option price of $3.20. The market price of RPJ stock three weeks ago was $42.70. Today, RPJ stock is selling at $44.75 a share and the July 45 put is priced at $.80. What is the intrinsic value of your put contract?
explanation: Contract intrinsic value = 1(100)[Max($45 − 44.75, 0)] Contract intrinsic value = $25

question: You own ten put option contracts on XYZ stock with an exercise price of $25. What is the total intrinsic value of these contracts if XYZ stock is currently selling for $24.50 a share?
explanation: Intrinsic value of contracts = 10(100)[Max($25 − 24.50, 0)] Intrinsic value of contracts = $500

question: Three months ago, you purchased three put option contracts on WXX stock with a strike price of $60 and an option price of $.60. The option expires today when the value of WXX stock is $48.10. Ignoring trading costs and taxes, what is your total profit on your investment?

Three months ago, you purchased a put option contract on WXX stock with a strike price of $61 and an option price of $.60. The option expires today when the value of WXX stock is $63.50. Ignoring trading costs and taxes, what is your total profit on your investment?

You sold ten put option contracts on PLT stock with an exercise price of $31.20 and an option price of $1.20. Today, the option expires and the underlying stock is selling for $33 a share. Ignoring trading costs and taxes, what is your total profit on this investment?

You sold a put contract on EDF stock at an option price of $.50 and an exercise price of $21. Today, EDF stock is selling for $20 a share and your option position was closed out. Ignoring transaction costs and taxes, what is your total profit?

You wrote ten call option contracts on JIG stock with a strike price of $41 and an option price of $.60. What is your total profit on this investment if the price of JIG is $46.05 on the option expiration date?

You wrote ten put option contracts on JIG stock with a strike price of $40 and an option price of $.40. What is your total profit on this investment if the price of JIG is $41.05 on the option expiration date?
explanation: The buyer of the put contracts will not exercise the options since the market price exceeds the strike price. Thus, your total profit equals the option premium you received as the option writer. Total profit = 10(100)[$.40 + MIN($41.05 − 40.00, 0)] Total profit = $400

question: You sold ten put option contracts on PLT stock with an exercise price of $32.50 and an option price of $1.10. Today, the option expires when the underlying stock is selling for $34.30 a share. Ignoring trading costs and taxes, what is your total profit on this investment?

You sold a put contract on EDF stock at an option price of $.25 and an exercise price of $22.50. The option expires today when EDF stock is selling for $21.70 a share. Ignoring transaction costs and taxes, what is your total profit on this investment?
explanation: Total profit = 1(100)[$.25 + MIN($21.70 − 22.50,0)] Total profit = −$55 When the put was exercised, you had to buy at $22.50 and could only resell at $21.70.

question: What is the cost of four October 40 put option contracts on a stock given the following price quotes? Stock price $40.86 Expiration: Strike Call bid Call ask Put bid Put ask Jul 35
What is the value of one August 25 call option contract? KNJ (KNJ) Underlying stock price: 30.86 Call Put Expiration Strike Last Last Aug 25

The market price of ABC stock has been very volatile and you think this volatility will continue for several weeks. Thus, you decide to purchase one two-month call option contract on ABC stock with a strike price of $25 and an option price of $1.30. You also purchase one two- month put option on ABC stock with a strike price of $25 and an option price of $.50. What will be your total profit on these positions if the stock price is $25.60 on the day the options expire?

Several rumors concerning Wyslow stock have started circulating. These rumors are causing the market price of the stock to become increasingly volatile. Given this situation, you decide to buy both a one-month put and a one-month call option on this stock with an exercise price of $15. You purchased the call at a quoted price of $.20 and the put at a price of $2.10. What will be your total profit on these option positions if the stock price is $6 on the day the options expire?

Tru-U stock is selling for $41 a share. A 6-month call on Tru-U stock with a strike price of $45 is priced at $1.60. Risk-free assets are currently returning .29 percent per month. What is the price of a 6-month put on Tru-U stock with a strike price of $45?
explanation: Using put-call parity: S + P = C + PV(E) P = $1.60 + ($45/1.00296) − $41 P = $4.82

question: GSX stock is selling for $32.40 a share. A 4-month call on GSX stock with a strike price of $35 is priced at $.55. Risk-free assets are currently returning .3 percent per month. What is the price of a 4-month put on GSX stock with a strike price of $35?
explanation: Using put-call parity: S + P = C + PV(E) P = $.55 + ($35/1.0034) − $32.40 P = $2.73

question: J&L stock has a current market price of $47.60 a share. The one-year call on this stock with a strike price of $45 is priced at $3.20 while the one-year put with a strike price of $45 is priced at $.15. What is the risk-free rate of return?

options.A: 2.71 percent
options.B: 1.76 percent
options.E: 2.87 percent

question: The market price of ABC stock has been very volatile and you think this volatility will continue for a few weeks. Thus, you decide to purchase a one-month call option contract on this stock with a strike price of $25 and an option price of $1.50. You also purchase a one-month put option on this stock with a strike price of $25 and an option price of $.70. What will be your total profit on these option positions if the stock price is $24.60 on the day the options expire?

Due to technological and market developments, the price of Beta stock has become quite volatile. Given this, you decide to buy two one-month put option contracts and two one-month call option contracts on this stock with an exercise price of $15. You purchased the calls at a quoted price of $.40 and the puts at a price of $2.30. What will be your total profit on these positions if the stock price is $29 on the day the options expire?

You own a call option on Jasper Co. stock that expires in one year. The exercise price is $35. The current price of the stock is $48 and the risk-free rate of return is 4.5 percent. Assume the option will finish in the money. What is the current value of the call option per share?

You currently own a one-year call option on WOI stock with a strike price of $20. The current stock price is $22.10 and the risk-free rate of return is 3.75 percent. If you assume the option finishes in the money, what is the current value of your call option per share?

The common stock of Mercury Motors is selling for $28.97 a share while one-year U.S. Treasury securities are currently yielding 2.8 percent. What is the current value per share of a one-year call option on Mercury Motors stock if the exercise price is $22.50 and you assume the option will finish in the money?

The common stock of WIN is currently priced at $52.50 a share. One year from now, the stock price is expected to be either $54 or $60 a share. The risk-free rate of return is 4 percent. What is the per share value of one call option on WIN stock with an exercise price of $55?

You own one call option with an exercise price of $30 on Nadia stock. This stock is currently selling for $27.80 a share but is expected to increase to either $28 or $34 a share over the next year. The risk-free rate of return is 5 percent. What is the current per share value of your option if it expires in one year?

The assets of Green Light Specials are currently worth $2,100. These assets are expected to be worth either $1,800 or $2,300 one year from now. The company has a pure discount bond outstanding with a $2,000 face value and a maturity date of one year. The risk-free rate is 5 percent. What is the value of the equity in this firm?
explanation: Delta = ($300 − 0)/($2,300 − 1,800) Delta = .60 Equity is a call option on a firm's assets, so: Value of call = $2,100(.60) − (.60)($1,800/1.05) Value of call = $231.43

question: Big Ed's Electrical has a pure discount bond that comes due in one year and has a face value of $1,000. The risk-free rate of return is 4 percent. The assets of Big Ed's are expected to be worth either $800 or $1,300 in one year. Currently, these assets are worth $1,140. What is the current value of the debt of Big Ed's Electrical?
explanation: Delta = ($300 − 0)/($1,300 − 800) Delta = .60 Equity is a call option on a firm's assets, so: Value of call = $1,140(.60) − (.60)($800/1.04) Value of call = $222.46 Value of debt = $1,140 − 222.46 Value of debt = $917.54

question: Martha B's has total assets of $1,810. These assets are expected to increase in value to either $1,900 or $2,400 by next year. The company has a pure discount bond outstanding with a face value of $2,000. This bond matures in one year. Currently, U.S. Treasury bills are yielding 5.5 percent. What is the value of the equity in this firm?
explanation: Delta = ($400 − 0)/($2,400 − 1,900) Delta = .80 Equity is a call option on a firm's assets, so: Value of call = $1,810(.80) − (.80)($1,900/1.055) Value of call = $7.24

5% Standard deviation 18% d1 .108367
Given the following information, what is the value of d2 as it is used in the Black-Scholes model? Stock price $ 36 Exercise price $ 40 Time of expiration .25 Risk-free rate
75% Standard deviation 26% d1 −.673350

What is the value of a call given the Black-Scholes model and the following information? Stock price = $44, Exercise price = $40, Time to expiration = .75, Risk-free rate = 4.5%, Standard deviation = 25%, N(d1) = .759395, and N(d2) = .687172.
Assume the delta of a call option on a firm's assets is .608. This means a project valued at $65,000 will increase the value of the firm's equity by:
explanation: Increase in equity value = $65,000(.608) Increase in equity value = $39,520

question: The current market value of the assets of Bigelow Inc. is $91 million, with a standard deviation of 19 percent per year. The firm has zero-coupon bonds outstanding with a total face value of $45 million. These bonds mature in 2 years. The risk-free rate is 4 percent per year compounded continuously. What is the value of d1?
The current market value of the assets of ABCD is $86.28 million. The call option value on the firm's assets is $53.09 million. What is the market value of the firm's debt?

options.A: $74.49 million
options.B: $31.36 million
options.E: $139.37 million

explanation: Market value of debt = $86.28m − 53.09m Market value of debt = $33.19m

question: Suppose your wealthy Aunt Minnie has asked you to manage her large stock portfolio. You would like to use options to increase her total return and also reduce her risks. Describe the types of options you would buy or sell, as well as your rationale, given the following circumstances: ∙ Aunt Minnie owns 10,000 shares of a large oil company common stock. You believe the stock is overpriced, but she won't let you sell any shares because her late husband told her to never, ever sell any. How do you protect her from what you believe is an impending price decline? ∙ Your analysis suggests that a technology stock is poised for a large price increase within the next year. Aunt Minnie won't agree to spend the dollars required to obtain shares but has consented to allow you to spend some money on options but only because she trusts you. You don't want to disappoint her. What should you do? Answer: ∙ Since you expect the stock to decline in value, you need a means of offsetting this potential loss. Assuming the market has not already priced in your expectation, you could buy puts now and then sell the puts prior to expiration. If your prediction is correct, you will earn a profit on the puts which will help offset the loss of value in the shares. If your prediction is incorrect and the stock increases in value, then you lose the option premium which will offset some of the gains in the stock value. You most likely would not want to write covered calls as you would risk the option being called if your prediction was incorrect and the stock rose in value. ∙ In this case, you would probably buy calls and hope to sell them at a higher price later. You could also write puts, but you would risk being forced to buy shares at the exercise price and sell them at a lower market price. In the latter case, Aunt Minnie would not be happy with you. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 22.2 Call Options Topic: Options Bloom's: Analyze AACSB: Analytical Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 50 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Suppose you look in the newspaper and see ABC trading at $50 per share. Calls on ABC with one month to expiration and an exercise price of $45 are trading at $6.50 each. Puts on ABC with one month to expiration and an exercise price of $55 are trading at $3.50 each. Are these prices reasonable? Explain. (Ignore transactions costs.) Answer: The calls are okay since the intrinsic value of each is $5 and the calls are trading at a price greater than this. However, the intrinsic value of the puts is $5, but the put is trading at $3.50. Thus, you could engage in arbitrage by buying puts for $3.50 each, exercising and selling the shares at the exercise price of $55, and purchasing shares to cover the sale in the market at $50 each, netting a profit of $150 per option contract. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 22.6 Combinations of Options Topic: Option valuations and payoffs Bloom's: Analyze AACSB: Analytical Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Suppose XYZ is priced at $125 a share. The 150 call has six months to expiration and is quoted at $.05. Why do you suppose investors would be willing to purchase a call that is so far out of the money? Answer: Options have both an intrinsic value and a time value. With six months left to maturity, there is a chance that the option could be in the money at some point during that time period. However, with the option premium only being $.05, investors aren't overly confident the stock price will exceed $150 within the next six months. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 22.7 Valuing Options Topic: Option valuation fundamentals Bloom's: Analyze AACSB: Analytical Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 51 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

What are the upper and lower bounds of an American call option? Explain what would happen in each case if the bound was violated. Answer: The upper bound on a call is the stock price. If the call price exceeded the stock price, you would be paying more for the option to buy an asset than the asset itself costs. The lower bound: C = 0 if S − E < 0 and C = (S − E) if (S − E) > 0. In the first case, if the call exercise price exceeds the stock price, the call is out of the money and it will either be worthless or have a time premium. In the second case, if the call is in the money, the call must be worth at least the difference between the asset's value and the exercise price. If the call was worth less than this value, rational investors would purchase calls, immediately exercise them, and then sell the stock at the current market price, completing an arbitrage. If the stock price equals the exercise price, then the call is at the money and will have a value of zero or an amount equal to the time premium. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 22.7 Valuing Options Topic: Option valuation fundamentals Bloom's: Analyze AACSB: Analytical Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Explain the rationale behind the statement that equity is a call option on the firm's assets. When would a shareholder allow the call to expire? Answer: The analogy only applies to levered firms. At maturity of the firm's debt, the stockholders have the option to either 1) pay the debtholders the par value of their debt or 2) turn the firm's assets over to the debtholders. If the firm's assets are worth less than the par value of the debt, the stockholders will not exercise their call, that is, they will let the debtholders have the assets. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 22.9 Stocks and Bonds as Options Topic: Equity and options Bloom's: Analyze AACSB: Analytical Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 52 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

How do options apply to capital budgeting? Explain and provide an example. Answer: There are many ways options apply in capital budgeting. One example goes back to the value of the firm and the leverage in the capital structure. For a highly levered firm, equity holders could prefer a lower NPV, or even a negative NPV project to a higher NPV project due to the low level of equity interest in the firm as a whole. Options apply in capital budgeting in many other ways such as delaying a project or a portion thereof, abandoning the project if it appears to be unsuccessful, or expanding the project if it is successful. While these particular options may not have been discussed in the chapter, you should realize that any option that provides flexibility increases the value of the project to the stockholders.
